steps.text: Refer questions and issues relating to gifts from foreign governments, international organizations, or outside organizations to the Human Resources Office (HRO), CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity.

preconditions.text: If questions and issues arise relating to gifts from foreign governments, international organizations, or outside organizations.

constraints.text: Gifts from foreign governments or international organizations, if authorized by the agency, are subject to the Foreign Gifts and Decorations Act (5 U.S.C. Section 7342).
Gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government are subject to the government-wide ethics regulations.

steps.text: Accept monetary gifts, subject to limitations imposed by applicable federal law, HHS or CDC policies, or the donor.
Accept monetary gifts in lieu of gifts in kind when feasible.

preconditions.text: Ensure the conditions imposed by the donor are not illegal, contrary to public policy, unreasonable to administer, contrary to generally accepted public standards, or an actual or apparent conflict of interest.

constraints.text: An agency may not augment its appropriation from an outside source without specific statutory authority to do so.

Any official or agent of the Government receiving money for the Government from any source shall deposit the money in the general fund of the Treasury as soon as practicable, unless the funds constitute an authorized repayment or the agency has specific statutory authority to retain the funds.

steps.text: Determine whether acceptance of a gift would compromise or appear to compromise the integrity of CDC or any of its employees, require CDC to undertake activities unrelated to its mission, or exert influence over its priorities.
Consider the identity of the immediate donor and, if applicable, the identity of any entity that funds the donor.
Review the gift for conflicts of interest.

constraints.text: Gifts supporting activities of individual employees are acceptable only if the principal beneficiary of the gift is CDC rather than the employee and the gift is given in such a form that the money can be used even if the employee leaves CDC.

steps.text: Conduct a conflict-of-interest review of the potential donor prior to accepting the gift (CDC Foundation).
Provide CDC with relevant information about the donor.
Conduct own conflict of interest review (CDC).
Accept non-monetary gift.

preconditions.text: The total costs to CDC associated with acceptance must not be expected to exceed the cost of purchasing a similar item and the cost of normal care and maintenance.

constraints.text: CDC may not direct the CDC Foundation to make a gift to CDC.
The donor is responsible for any valuation of gifts.

steps.text: Perform a balancing test to determine whether the benefits outweigh the risk that accepting the gift would reflect unfavorably upon CDC.
Review, determine, and keep records of non-monetary gifts for projects within the CIO.
Document the review, the donor, and a description of the in-kind gift.
Consider what the monetary value of a gift would be, if possible, and record that value in review documentation.
Consult with OGC.
Request a CDC COI Panel Review.

preconditions.text: If a CIO makes a determination that there is a potential conflict of interest concern
If the potential conflict of interest concern requires further evaluation

constraints.text: The gift should not be accepted if any employee’s ability to carry out duties is influenced, if acceptance compromises integrity, or if the donor expects a future benefit.
CDC may not accept a gift if it is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be met from the gift itself or from the income of the gift.
Neither the gift nor any conditions associated with the gift should exert undue influence over CDC programs and priorities.

steps.text: Complete the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey to determine whether the gift is acceptable to the CIO.
Submit the gift for evaluation by the CDC Conflict of Interest Review Panel.

preconditions.text: A prohibited source offers a gift.

constraints.text: The CIO gift review official must be especially aware of the increased probability that an actual or apparent conflict of interest may exist.

steps.text: Review recommendations regarding excluded sources made by the Advisory Committee to the Director in its May 2016 report.
Contact OFR, Office of Debt Management for disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to the agency.
Contact the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity for guidance regarding disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to individual employees.
Return tangible items to the donor.
Pay the donor market value (retail cost of an item or service of like quality).
Destroy, share within the office, or donate items.

preconditions.text: There is any question that a potential donor may be an excluded source.
A gift cannot be accepted.
OGC approves the payment as a legally permissible use of appropriated funds.
A supervisor or organization head approves the destruction, sharing, or donation of items.

constraints.text: Written notification must accompany all gift returns.

CDC will not accept a gift that is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be covered by the gift itself or from income earned from the gift, unless an Act of Congress has approved such expenditure.
Offers of property will not be accepted if the total costs associated with acceptance are expected to exceed the cost of purchasing a similar item and the cost of normal care and maintenance.
Gifts may not be accepted if the conditions placed on the gift create a conflict of interest.

steps.text: Obtain pre-approval from OFR for gift fund expenditures on entertainment.
Expend gift funds for official entertainment.
Consult with the OFR or the Ethics and Compliance Activity Office for assistance with sponsored travel.
Meet all statutory requirements regarding the acceptance of conditional gifts (grants).
Use CDC's appropriated funds to cover costs incurred in applying for grants.

preconditions.text: The entertainment furthers a valid, authorized function of CDC, could not be otherwise accomplished without the expenditure, and does not violate donor restrictions.
The expenditure meets the necessary expense test and has a legitimate official purpose.
There must be no legal objections to CDC's acceptance of the grants, and grant funds must be used to advance CDC's mission.

constraints.text: Donated funds may not be used for entertainment that does not bear a legitimate official purpose, such as an office holiday party.
Once received by CDC, gift funds become public funds and should be spent in the same manner as appropriated funds.
Sponsored travel is not covered by this policy.

steps.text: Determine whether the funds are intended for CDC or for the employee serving in an individual capacity.
Become familiar with this policy.
Follow the processes provided in this document to respond to a gift made to CDC.
Contact the Office of the General Counsel, the Office of Financial Resources, or the CIO CDC Foundation Liaison.
Direct questions regarding agency ethics issues to OGC.

preconditions.text: If you have questions regarding agency ethics issues

steps.text: Forward cash or cash equivalents for deposit to the Deputy Chief Financial Officer.
Accompany the gift transmittal with a brief memorandum stating that the gift is pre-decisional and include any accompanying documentation.
Mail gift fund checks to the designated USPS or Private Carrier address.
Transmit wired funds via pay.gov.
Deposit the gift in a suspense account (OFR).
Return checks more than 90 days old to the recipient organization for return to the donor for reissuance.
Determine if the proposed gift has been recommended for approval, whether to accept, and notify the donor of the determination.
Use the gift acceptance checklists and the gift acceptance validity survey for Prohibited Sources when making a decision on acceptance.
Submit checklists through OGC and OFR to the designated approving official for review and approval.
Review the proposal and provide legal advice on the administration of the gift to the approving official (OGC).
Ensure that the gift meets the criteria of the pre-acceptance checklist and that all supporting documentation is complete (DCFO).

preconditions.text: If the gift is a check
If the gift is wired funds
Unless the gift is officially accepted within the 24-hour period
If monetary gift checks are more than 90 days old
If the gift is less than $500, DCFO has authority to accept without CDC Conflict of Interest Panel Review

constraints.text: All offices must forward cash or cash equivalents within 24 hours of receipt.
OFR does not accept monetary gift checks more than 90 days old.

steps.text: Create a basic record in the on-line property system including estimated value, manufacturer's serial number, month/year received, physical location, CDC decal number, and donor purpose.
Determine at least annually if there will be excess funds not used within a 12-month period.
Request in writing that the Chief Financial Officer invest the excess funds in interest-bearing obligations of the United States.
Provide CIOs with quarterly status sheets of all invested funds, including amounts, dates of investment, and maturity.
Return the liquidated monies to the same gift fund account to be used for the benefit of the CIOs.

preconditions.text: Notification of acceptance of the gift by the CIO property custodian is received.
It is determined that there are excess funds and the amount of these funds is $50,000 or more.
Liquidation of investments occurs.

constraints.text: The director's memorandum must include the amount of funds invested, the source of funds, recommendations regarding purchase, and when investments will be redeemed.
If an investment is redeemed before the maturity date, there may be loss of principal.
The spend plan must be aligned with the investment of the securities and redemption of the securities.
Gift funds must be received prior to obligation or expenditure.

steps.text: Determine if the donor is an acceptable source.
Determine if the gift meets generally acceptable public standards.
Determine if the gift is reasonable to administer.
Determine if the principle beneficiary of the gift will be CDC.

preconditions.text: The donor is an acceptable source (Yes).
The gift meets generally acceptable public standards (Yes).
The gift is reasonable to administer (Yes).

steps.text: Determine if there is an actual or apparent conflict of interest.
Determine if the gift was solicited by a CDC employee.
Determine if CDC has an interest in accepting the gift.
Determine if the size or the nature of the gift alone will raise a significant concern.
Determine if the gift is offered to support the activities of an individual employee.
Determine if accepting the gift will reflect unfavorably on the ability of CDC or any employee to carry out responsibilities or official duties in a fair and objective manner.
Determine if the gift is offered for endorsement purposes (No. 14).
Determine if there are donor-imposed restrictions or conditions (No. 15).
Determine if there is sensitivity in any matter pending before CDC that would affect the interest of the donor (No. 16).
Answer Question 17: Determine if there are any effects of accepting the gift on entities inside or outside the agency or component, and to what extent.
Answer Question 18: Consult the Office of the General Counsel to provide guidance on problematic issues about the acceptance of the gift.
Answer Question 19: Determine the practical impact of the gift within CDC or CIO.
Deposit the funds in the (Name of CIO or OD component) Conditional Gift Fund Account.
Use the funds to support (Name of specific project and/or researcher).
Deposit the unexpended balance in the (Name of CIO or OD component) Unconditional Gift Fund.
Sign and return a copy of the letter to acknowledge acceptance of the condition.
Draft the Letter of Acceptance for Unconditional Gifts.

preconditions.text: Unobligated funds remain in the account after completion of the project.

constraints.text: By signing and returning a copy of this letter, the donor acknowledges acceptance of the condition regarding excess funds.
Use CIO letterhead if CIO Director is the signature authority.
